content: a deep dive into engineering coding harnesses. And coding harnesses matter because even with the same model, you could have dramatically different performance differences. Also, there's lots of knobs inside of one of these coding harnesses that you can tweak to improve performance, improve your capabilities. And finally, there's the environment that you use these coding harnesses. And that can affect your freedom, your ability to protect your own private information as well. So, those are some of the reasons we want to go into it. Stepping back, may most of you remember the Transformer just doing something like sentiment with that. We'd use that model for a stateless query. Give it a movie review and ask it for its sentiment. It would take very quickly. It would use a few amount of tokens. Today, the tasks are dramatically different. Now, what we do is we give that same model a whole GitHub repo full of code and say, "Hey, there's a bug here. Here's some unit test. I want you to go out and fix it." And that model is going to go out there and it's going to work. It's not just going to do one pass. It actually will loop and cycle over and over through that. And so, this one problem could use something on the order of 13 million tokens for this particular example to generate 50,000 tokens of output. It'll take 20 minutes. Okay. Now, what's the harness, Raj? Well, remember what we want to do is support this looping process where we start with a prompt, we pass that to a model, the model performs some action, but then it takes feedback. And then based on that, it's going to modify its prompt, figure out how better to improve and solve that and go in a loop. Now, for this type of loop to work, this is where an effective harness comes into play. Because first of all, we have that system state that we have to keep track of, the repo, the memory. Now, we'll touch on all of these things as we go through this deep dive, but to give you the quick heads-up. There's a big safety piece. How we handle memory state. Now, often when we think about context, we have these like really large um context windows. Like 1 million, often most of the leading models have that. What I want to point out though is like 1 million is big, but like we have an insatiable amount of data, whether lots and lots of PDFs, huge repos, it's never enough. And then more importantly, these windows degrade. As you stuff more and more information into them, they lose track of that information. They can't keep track of it. So, this is where you can run the experiments yourself. I put a link in my Harness Engineering um repo, which has lots of fun experiments like this, but these models literally drop information in those long contexts. And this can be a problematic if you have for example a tool that maybe pushes off a ton of error messages that just sucks up your context window. Everyday developers often know that say, you know, don't go over half the context window. You're going to start losing information. You should only use a portion of that piece. So, uh It It sucks that we have these long context windows, but we can't fully use them. This is where layers of memory come into play. I'm going to break this up into three different layers. So, one is How do we actually work with the existing context window for the for your model that you're working with? >> [snorts] >> Next is how can we create something temporary, something working state, some files that help us kind of supplement the memory? And finally, we'll talk about durable memory. Things that we will save for a long time that we can use across sessions as a way to improve the memory. Let's start with working with the act active context window. So, one thing we can do is just manage that context window. So, when we were doing multi-turn chat for example, how we'd have it just have a sliding window and only remember the most recent pieces like the last three um messages back and forth and drop off the others, right? That's a very common technique we would use to manage that context window. Other things that you can do besides that is let's summarize or compact older information. So, that way what we're doing is throwing away the stuff that isn't useful and just saving condensing down the really important pieces. Right? If we do this now, the context window is much smaller. So, when I do an API call, I don't need as many tokens and so that can save me money. And at Open Hands, we've run our tests and found out that if you use a compacting strategy effectively, not only can you save money, you can still get similar or even sometimes a little bit better performance on software engineering tasks. And this compaction we see widely through lots of different coding harnesses. The thing is is some of these coding harnesses like Codex, you got no idea why. They have some type of prompt that they don't tell you what they're using for compaction, then they encrypt everything on one of their like servers in the cloud. So, all of that stuff that's yours, you have no idea exactly how they're doing it. And remember, if they make a mistake, they can throw out some important information that affects you downstream later on your tasks. Um the other thing is is Claude code is really good at having a number of different techniques for managing the context window. One of my favorite is rewind. You kind of hit escape twice, where you could actually go back a couple of steps, and that way you're not if you've gone down the wrong branch, you're not filling your context window with that wrong branch. You're actually able to go back and rewind. Very useful technique. The second approach is how we can use files to make a better memory. The Not just saving everything in context, but writing out of for example a plan, and writing that down to a markdown, and we use that and refer to that markdown file as we work through. LangChain's deep agents were really good at this. I remember it I think I have done earlier videos where you could see literally how they check off their to-do's as they're working through the process like that. And all the way to the extreme are these recursive language models. Where now what they say is, "Hey, let's just save everything that we've done, and then we're going to use a Python REPL to search and pull that stuff." And so it effectively gives us a really big context window cuz I can use stuff from really far in the past, search and retrieve that. And when they tested this out, they were able to test this with long context tasks and get much better performance than without when then using this technique. Finally, there's durable memory. So, this type of memory really can supplement the model even have it so we don't need the code like that. Now, these skills work as a kind of a progressive memory. They slowly can start being used by the models, but we can also improve these skills as well. So, one of the things that deeper you have to think about and um Harrison Chase over at LangChain has talked heavily about this is when you're using some of these closed harnesses, where all of your data, your memories, how it's summarizations had, the skills, they're living in somebody else's environment. They're being processed by those proprietary approaches. That stuff is locked away from you versus using an open harness where you know exactly what the compaction strategy is. And 01 was really the start of these kind of test time inference having these models that just weren't doing a single shot that were kind of rethinking through their chain of thought and improving in what they were what their results were. And nowadays, you'll see something like Opus already comes with a bunch of tools in there. And the expectation is that you're going to be working in a looping way with tools like that. A simple kind of baseline loop is the Ralph Wiggum agent. This is comes from the Simpsons character, which wasn't the brightest guy, which would do the same thing over and over again without learning at all. And that's the key here. Is the essence of this loop is that you try something. If it doesn't work, it's okay. You just keep trying. That's it. You don't you don't even try to learn. Just wipe everything and restart. And this is a very brute force approach. You could end up using a lots lots of tokens to do this. I Ray on our team kind of built a C compiler this way. I I've linked to this other blog post that was able to port lots of code over in this particular way. It can work, but again, you're using lots of tokens to do this. A better way to do this is to have a plan before you execute and make sure you're sticking according to the plan to kind of keep things moving along in thing. This is probably most of us are already doing this by default. Karpathy's auto research is another step along this lines where he used an agent to go out and research how to improve the performance of a transformer in training by running lots of different experiments. And you can see over time, it steadily got better and better. And the key here was having a loop where there was a hypothesis and a verification step. The model would think, "Hey, what's the best strategy I should use?" Then based on the results, "Oh, this worked, this didn't work. I'm going to feed that back into my next approach." And that way the model was able to learn, make that tighter improved loop like that. Now, with harnesses, a good harness is going to have pieces like things are going to go wrong, tools are going to output a massive amount of junk information, for example. Having a harness that knows that's smart enough to, "Hey, let's truncate and only keep the last 2,000 lines, not all 20,000 lines that were out." Seems very simple, but these types of things save your context window are important. Another piece is that's working with these coding agents is test-driven development. These coding agents work really effectively if you give it tests that it can check as it's working through these loops to see, is it really it has it really solved the problem it's supposed to? Now, the folks over at Factory at a recent talk reminded us that when you write these tests don't write the application and then write the test. Because what you can do is actually kind of rubber stamp some assumptions that were in the code in your test. Instead, what you want to do is make sure you write the tests first and then kind of build the code. That gives you a little bit better perspective on that piece. The folks over at OpenAI have also talked quite a bit about kind of this development process and how and how it's changing because with these coding models, it's very easy to get code. It's thinking about what is the architecture? How should we set up the problem? Is really the hard part. And they talk about how we're moving from developers to much more managers of these systems. And part of that is that we should add system constraints into this as part of the instructions. We should for example have instructions that tell it don't have files over 200 lines as a way to make sure it's kind of constantly decomposing itself and not building massive pieces like that. The last piece is we need some we need safety and friction built in. We've seen the horror stories, right? Working on your laptop is not a great idea. You have lots of API keys, lots of information that are available to an agent on the key that can cause a problem. You're much better off working having your code running in an isolated sandbox as a way to kind of reduce the chance of something to go wrong. Beyond that um beyond [clears throat] that, we also want to think about having some type of guardrails or kind of approval friction is kind of what I like to call it. So that way there's something that are great, it's okay to do, no no concerns at all. But there's some things that are >> [clears throat] >> let's you should be paying a little bit of attention, right? And some things that are all really destructive like, "Whoa, are you really sure that you're wanting to do that?" And you want your coding harness to support that kind of friction to make sure it's building useful stuff for you. Now, when we talk about these loops, I think these principles that we saw back from the SWE bench paper still really hold true. That we want as we work with our agents, we want actions that are simple and easy for agents to understand, that are compact, efficient. Environmental feedback is great, but it has to be informative and concise. And then we need guardrails, right? To help mitigate error propagation, improve kind of recovery from issues as well. Still doesn't mean that we don't shouldn't use multi-agents. Many of us in many coding harnesses by default support these types of multi-agent systems, but these are a little bit more constrained where maybe I want to have parallelization. I'm doing something, but if I can split that task into five pieces, we can get everybody done. Or I'm going to use an orchestrator agent where I have one agent in charge, and it's going to go and talk to several other agents to be able to perform actions as well. So, many of the coding harnesses, for example, will do things like subtasks and manage that for you within the coding harness when you're working. Now, I see this all the time on um on social media, how people talk about how I'm going to just unleash a hundred agents in parallel to go solve all the coding issues in this repo and kind of run this big large parallel swarm. Here's the thing, like all those agents have to be able to coordinate and talk to each other. And Factory at a recent talk shared like how that was just troublesome for them, that they found that having kind of a serial approach where you have orchestrators, workers, a validation, like that approach made much more sense and was much more useful for them than just having lots of coding agents running in parallel. And this is one of the studies I've seen, and it's been kind of my favorite that looked at a lot of popular benchmarks and found on average that single agents were better than a multi-agent system. Because for multi-agents to really work effectively, they have to be able to coordinate with it with each other, and that's a large coordination tax that can be difficult to do. So, think carefully about kind of how you're going to build this multi-agent. You shouldn't reflexively think that multi-agents are going to solve things um for you.
